topic: Dialogues between Philosophy & Religion / Ethical Studies & Religion
subject: religious-studies

items.q: Does 'dialogue' mean you must prove religion either true or false?
items.correct: No - it means examining how the religion responds to philosophical and ethical challenges; responses may strengthen, adapt or resist belief, and your task is evaluation.
items.misconception: Dialogues questions want students to demonstrate that religion is false (or true).
items.random_wrong: Dialogues questions are about conversations between two religious leaders.
items.why: Students read 'challenge' as 'refutation'; the assessment rewards balanced weighing, not a predetermined verdict.

items.q: Are philosophy and religion automatically opposed to each other?
items.correct: No - there are long traditions of using reason within faith, such as Aquinas's natural theology; 'faith versus reason' is one position in the debate, not an established fact.
items.misconception: Philosophy and religion are necessarily enemies, so dialogue is impossible.
items.random_wrong: Philosophy was invented to replace religion in the twentieth century.
items.why: The conflict framing is popular but ahistorical; recognising cooperative models is essential for dialogue essays.

items.q: Is describing both topics side by side enough for high marks?
items.correct: No - dialogues demand genuine connection and evaluation: showing how the philosophical or ethical material bears on the religion's claims and judging the outcome.
items.misconception: Writing everything you know about both topics earns the evaluation marks.
items.random_wrong: Only quotations from scripture can earn marks in dialogues answers.
items.why: Juxtaposition without analysis is the most common weakness; AO2 marks come from argued connection.

items.q: What does the 'dialogues' element of the course require you to do?
items.correct: Relate philosophy of religion and ethical studies to the teachings of the religion you have studied
items.misconception: Memorise the practices of six world religions
items.random_wrong: Study secular philosophy with no reference to religion
items.why: Correct answer: Relate philosophy of religion and ethical studies to the teachings of the religion you have studied. “Memorise the practices of six world religions” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which question is an example of a dialogue between philosophy and religion?
items.correct: How far does the problem of evil challenge this religion's understanding of God?
items.misconception: In what year was a particular church council held?
items.random_wrong: How many chapters are in a sacred text?
items.why: It's How far does the problem of evil challenge this religion's understanding of God?. “In what year was a particular church council held?” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which question is an example of a dialogue between ethical theory and religion?
items.correct: Is situation ethics compatible with the religion's moral teaching?
items.misconception: What is the population of religious believers worldwide?
items.random_wrong: When was the religion's founder born?
items.why: Correct answer: Is situation ethics compatible with the religion's moral teaching?. “What is the population of religious believers worldwide?” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What makes a strong evaluative answer in dialogues questions?
items.correct: Weighing arguments for and against compatibility and reaching a justified judgement
items.misconception: Listing as many scholars as possible without comment
items.random_wrong: Asserting personal faith as conclusive proof
items.why: Correct answer: Weighing arguments for and against compatibility and reaching a justified judgement. “Listing as many scholars as possible without comment” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
